info.title: Benchmark Validity Assessment Stage 3 (expert_b81915e01b62@anonymous.invalid)
info.description:
Thank you for participating in this expert validation study.

You will review automated validity assessments for benchmark datasets in specific deployment contexts. For each assessment, you will:

1. Read a companion PDF with the detailed analysis
2. Answer questions about the assessment's accuracy and completeness

Each section includes a link to the relevant PDF — please open it before answering the questions.

Estimated time: 2.5 - 5 hours.

IMPORTANT: The form has 34 sections. Please aim to take 5-10 minutes on each one.

Please be as transparent as possible with your answers. This will help us accurately frame any contributions or uses of the pipeline in the paper, as well as help us improve the pipeline for future iterations.

IMPORTANT: The total PDF materials are quite long due to the inclusion of detailed evidence tables. You should aim to fully digest the main text of each document, but you do not need to exhaustively read the evidence tables — they are provided as a reference to help you assess your answers to the questions in this form.

Your responses are saved automatically as you progress (requires being signed into your Google account; drafts are tied to your browser and device).

items.title: Assessment 1: AFRIDOC_MT — Ethiopia Ministry of Health — Amharic Clinical Translation Deployment
items.description:
Use case and domain: Translating clinical practice guidelines, vaccination schedules, and maternal-health booklets into Amharic for ministry-of-health distribution
Target population: Health workers and patients across Ethiopian regional health bureaus producing and consuming Amharic-language material

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark covers health documents, but clinical practice guidelines, vaccination schedules, and maternal-health booklets have specific structural features — numbered protocols, dosage tables, immunization timetables, warning boxes, and administrative forms. Does your deployment need to handle these structured document types, or is it primarily narrative/prose health content? Are there other document formats common in Ethiopian ministry-of-health materials that you'd consider critical to cover?"
  → "Since the benchmark is sourced from WHO articles it will be difficult to expect deployment need to include other document formats, so i think it would be nice if primarily focus  health content."

Q2 (IC): "Ethiopian clinical and maternal-health documents often reference locally specific content: Ethiopian calendar dates (e.g., vaccination schedules tied to Meskerem or Tikimt), endemic diseases prevalent in Ethiopia (e.g., malaria zones by region, kala-azar), local nutrition guidance, or Ethiopia-specific referral pathways. Would your source documents contain this kind of Ethiopia-specific clinical content, and would a system trained on more generic health text be expected to handle it correctly?"
  → "Since the benchmark is only sourced from WHO articles it might not contain local specific contents, however most models are expected to preform in general translation tasks like translating dates. For Endemic disease i believe models trained on generic health texts are able to translate the contents to target language like Amharic"

Q3 (OC): "For ground-truth translation quality in your context, whose judgment should be authoritative — clinical translators trained by the Ministry of Health, Amharic-speaking physicians, community health extension workers, or patients? Would terminology choices that are acceptable to an Addis Ababa-based medical translator be trusted equally by health workers in Tigray, Oromia, or Somali regional bureaus, or do regional Amharic registers and health terminology vary enough to matter?"
  → "Since health domain is very sensitive domain,  ground truth translation by clinical translators trained by the Ministry of Health should be authoritative but incase if we don't have specific trained by MOH i think we can also consider using translations by Amharic-speaking physicians since they are familiar with the domain and speaks the target language."

Q4 (OO): "Clinical translation quality can be scored in different ways — faithfulness to source meaning, terminological consistency across a full document (e.g., the same drug name every time it appears), readability for a semi-literate patient, or compliance with Ministry of Health-approved glossaries. Which of these does your team treat as the primary success criterion, and does your evaluation process use a fixed approved glossary that translations must conform to?"
  → "I believe it is important to consider different metrics in addition to  using automatic metrics known in MT evaluation. I would consider results automatic metrics  (MT), faithfulness to source meaning, terminological consistency and compliance with Ministry of Health-approved glossaries for success criteria. There are approved glossaries by Ministry of Health (MOH) of Ethiopia and  Ethiopian Food and Drug Authority (EFDA) in Ethiopia if needed."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1eHOILkWuVuimC8QkD0GrrfjeUru6VBPM/view?usp=drivesdk

items.title: Are there specific refinements or additions that would make this assessment more useful for practitioners in your domain?
Assessment 2: AFRIDOC_MT — Amhara Region Agricultural & Microfinance Document Translation — Cooperative and Rural MFI End-Users
items.description:
Use case and domain: Document-level translation of agricultural input subsidy notices, land-use policy updates, and credit-scheme terms from federal and regional bureaus into Amharic
Target population: Farmer cooperative leaders and rural microfinance clients (e.g., ACSI, ADCSI borrowers) in Amhara interpreting government and lender document

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark covers health and IT news documents. Your deployment targets agricultural input subsidy notices, land-use policy updates, and credit-scheme terms — genres with distinctive bureaucratic phrasing, legal terminology, and numbered clause structures. Are there document types in your deployment that combine legal/regulatory language with agricultural or financial jargon in ways that differ substantially from health or tech reporting? For example, do your documents include itemized eligibility conditions, penalty clauses, or seasonal deadlines that require consistent terminology across long stretches of text?"
  → "Yes the benchmark only covers health and IT domains but we expect the models to preform in other document level machine translation tasks even if the benchmark don't cover agricultural domain. I don't think there is substantial difference when it comes to document structure  but there might be some terminologies that are only used in agricultural domains"

Q2 (IC): "Government and lender documents in Amhara often carry culturally specific concepts — such as cooperative membership tiers, traditional land-tenure categories (e.g., rist), or regionally named subsidy schemes (e.g., ATA-linked input packages). Would your target documents contain terminology like these that requires locally grounded Amharic equivalents rather than direct transliterations or generic terms? Are there concepts your cooperative leaders or microfinance clients would expect to see rendered in specific Amharic wordings they already know from prior government communications?"
  → "Yes i agree there might some culturally specific concepts but when it comes to agricultural domain most of the terms for example for land-tenure categories in Ethiopia we follow different proclamations from federal and regional office which replaced most of the local terminologies"

Q3 (OC): "For your deployment, who should be the authority on whether an Amharic translation of a subsidy notice or loan agreement is correct and trustworthy — federal bureau translators, regional bureau staff, cooperative extension workers, or the farmer leaders and borrowers themselves? Would Amhara cooperative leaders plausibly dispute a translation that is linguistically accurate but uses unfamiliar administrative vocabulary versus one that uses locally recognized phrasing, even if less formally precise?"
  → "I believe if we have translators that are familiar with the domain like  federal bureau translators, they should have authority but if we don't have people who are trained to do translation in the specific domain we can relay on other translators like regional bureau staff and cooperative extension workers."

Q4 (OF): "Your target users are cooperative leaders and rural microfinance clients interpreting binding government and lender documents. Do they need translated output that preserves the original document structure — numbered clauses, tables of subsidy rates, signature blocks — or is a flowing prose translation sufficient? Would a translation that reorders or collapses sections for readability be acceptable, or must it mirror the source layout exactly for legal and administrative compliance purposes?"
  → "I think it depends on the model's input-output modality. If the model is multimodal, we expect the model to keep formats including tables and others while translating, but if the model is text only, it is also acceptable if the model provide a flowing prose translation."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1CO_AYpmXSLuL5FZC_XWCxCumtNib6r1o/view?usp=drivesdk

items.title: Are there specific refinements or additions that would make this assessment more useful for practitioners in your domain?
Comparative 1: AFRIDOC_MT vs. NLLB
items.description:
The pipeline assessed two benchmarks for the same deployment context: AFRIDOC_MT (a benchmark proposed by you) and NLLB (https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.04672) (a benchmark we selected for comparison). Both were evaluated across the same six validity dimensions. The comparative PDF below presents excerpts from each benchmark's individual assessment for this context — please review it before answering the questions.

AFRIDOC_MT: 2.3/5 avg | NLLB: 2.7/5 avg
Context: Amhara Region Agricultural & Microfinance Document Translation — Cooperative and Rural MFI End-Users

▶ Open the Comparative PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1PzjWpFTjhjNrd0zaxe3vWhavSoCy-qQ7/view?usp=drivesdk

items.title: The pipeline's justifications convincingly explain why the two benchmarks received different scores.
The scoring leaves room to grow: a benchmark designed to address the gaps identified in both assessments could reasonably achieve a higher score for this deployment context.
Are there important differences between these two benchmarks (for this deployment context) that the pipeline failed to capture?
Assessment 3: INJONGO — Ethiopian Intercity/Ride-Hail Commuter Cohort (Amharic-Primary)
items.description:
Use case and domain: Amharic intercity-travel booking bots for Sky Bus, Selam Bus, and Ride covering routes, fares, and seat changes
Target population: Inter-regional commuters and travelers booking long-distance buses and ride-hail trips via smartphone apps

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "INJONGO covers a general 'travel' domain, but Sky Bus, Selam Bus, and Ride have very specific booking flows. Does your system need to handle intents specific to Ethiopian intercity travel that may not appear in a generic travel intent set — for example, querying routes between specific regional cities (e.g., Addis Ababa to Hawassa or Mekelle), booking during Ethiopian holidays like Timkat or Meskel when demand surges, or managing seat classes unique to these operators? Are there operator-specific intents (e.g., 'cancel and rebook due to road closures') that you'd expect users to express?"
  → "Yes the dataset covers general domains, however booking buses or ride sharing in Ethiopia has not that much different booking work flows than the others. We expect the system to handle all expected scenarios."

Q2 (IC): "For the slot-filling part of your booking bot, users will need to provide values like origin/destination cities, travel dates, and fare types. Ethiopian intercity travelers may refer to cities by local Amharic names or abbreviations, use the Ethiopian calendar for dates (e.g., Meskerem, Tikimt), and quote fares in Birr with locally familiar pricing tiers. Would your users' natural utterances reflect these conventions, and would your system need to correctly extract them as slot values?"
  → "Yes I believe the above listed conventions reflects users' natural utterances  and we expect the system to extract them correctly"

Q3 (OC): "INJONGO's Amharic utterances were generated by native speakers, but 'native Amharic speaker' covers a wide range — urban Addis residents, speakers from Amhara region, and Amharic-as-second-language speakers from other ethnic groups who also use intercity buses. For your specific commuter population, are there systematic differences in how users phrase travel requests (e.g., code-switching with Oromo or Tigrinya words for routes, regional city names) that might not be captured by a single annotator profile?"
  → "Yes the data is curated by native speakers, however we expect some code-switching in case of Amharic"

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1bsM8Ddt5iUobJvPSY6z9dsCu2zrB1Vjp/view?usp=drivesdk

Assessment 4: INJONGO — Urban Ethiopian Utility Chatbot Users — Amharic-Language Telegram/SMS
items.description:
Use case and domain: Amharic utility chatbots for Ethiopian Electric Utility and Addis Ababa Water handling prepaid meter top-ups, outage reports, and bill disputes
Target population: Urban households in Addis Ababa and regional capitals interacting with utility support over Telegram and SMS

Your elicitation responses:

Q1 (IO): "The benchmark covers a general 'utility' domain across many African contexts. For your Ethiopian Electric Utility and water chatbots specifically, does your system need to handle intents beyond what a pan-African utility dataset might include — for example, Ethiopia-specific prepaid meter codes (e.g., STS token entry), Addis Ababa Water's tiered pricing disputes, power-shedding schedules (ye'eletrik tirf), or informal neighborhood reporting through idirs? Are there utility workflows unique to Ethiopia that a broader African dataset might not capture?"
  → "I do not think there are many differences in Ethiopia, but there might be some differences when it comes to payment methods, and some specific issues tailored to Ethiopian use case scenarios."

Q2 (IC): "Your users interact over Telegram and SMS, which means their Amharic may include informal abbreviations, Ethiopic numerals mixed with Arabic numerals, code-switching with English or Oromo (especially in regional capitals like Dire Dawa or Hawassa), and SMS-constrained shorthand. Does the Amharic in your deployment look like this, and are there specific regional capital dialects or mixed-language patterns you'd expect the system to handle correctly?"
  → "Yes this is expected because especially code-switching is becoming more common in different part of Ethiopia. We expect the deployment to handle this kind of differences. In case of Amharic usually the speakers mix it english especially the younger generations so we expect more code-switching."

Q3 (OC): "For slot-filling in your utility context — extracting meter numbers, account IDs, addresses, and payment amounts — whose judgment determines correct extraction? For instance, would Ethiopian Electric Utility's customer service agents or Addis Ababa Water's billing staff serve as the authoritative validators, or are there community-level expectations (e.g., how customers refer to kebele addresses or informal neighborhood names) that differ from official records and could cause mismatches in ground-truth labels?"
  → "In Ethiopia, for utility related issues are handled by their respective customer service and/or agents,  For electric utility Ethiopian,  their customer service and/or agents judgments should determince correct extraction."

Q4 (IF): "Your deployment runs over Telegram and SMS. SMS channels impose character limits and may strip or corrupt Ethiopic script (Ge'ez), potentially forcing users to romanize Amharic or use numeric codes instead of natural language. How often do your users send messages in romanized Amharic or with degraded Ethiopic encoding on SMS versus full Ethiopic script on Telegram, and does your system need to handle both representations reliably?"
  → "Telegram is most popular app in Ethiopia and there are a lot of government and non-government organizations using it for different tasks. As far as I know users use full Ethiopic script , i would suggest only handle  full Ethiopic script  instead of romanized Amharic."

▶ Open the Summary PDF before answering: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1CIZWVMnLE8khF2cjJ1u4WshBUBn1B6zV/view?usp=drivesdk

Comparative 2: INJONGO vs. MASSIVE
items.description:
The pipeline assessed two benchmarks for the same deployment context: INJONGO (a benchmark proposed by you) and MASSIVE (https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.08582) (a benchmark we selected for comparison). Both were evaluated across the same six validity dimensions. The comparative PDF below presents excerpts from each benchmark's individual assessment for this context — please review it before answering the questions.

INJONGO: 3.0/5 avg | MASSIVE: 2.3/5 avg
Context: Ethiopian Intercity/Ride-Hail Commuter Cohort (Amharic-Primary)

▶ Open the Comparative PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1xk6tvNaleR4hR0am79Mu5otfzRWyaV9B/view?usp=drivesdk
